claim.statement: Selective attention under choice-contingent feedback can drive learners into ‘attentional learning traps,’ leading to persistent false beliefs and sub-optimal decisions even after extensive experience.
claim.statement_location: Abstract / Introduction section – overall summary of paper; reiterated in Discussion concluding that learning traps are stable false beliefs arising from interaction of selective attention and decision-making.
claim.study_type: Experimental (computer simulations and human laboratory experiments)

data.source: 1) Computer-generated simulation data using the ALCOVE-RL model; 2) Human participant data collected via Amazon Mechanical Turk.
data.wave_or_subset: Experiment 1 (bee task, 101 participants) and Experiment 2 (job-application task, 400 participants) plus five simulated conditions.
data.sample_size: Simulation: 1,000 runs per condition; Human experiments: 101 participants (Exp 1) and 400 participants (Exp 2).
data.notes: Study also analyses multiple informational conditions (contingent vs full-information) and three intervention subsets (individuation, occlusion, noise).

method.description: The authors combined large-scale computer simulations of an adapted ALCOVE reinforcement-learning model with two online behavioural experiments. Simulations manipulated feedback availability and attentional learning to test how selective attention and information structure generate learning traps. Human experiments replicated these conditions with visual classification (bee task) and job-application decisions, comparing contingent feedback to full information and to three debiasing interventions. Bayesian statistical models evaluated behavioural scores indicating one- or two-dimensional learning.
steps: Implement ALCOVE-RL model with parameters: specificity 6, temperature 15, learning rates 0.1 (weights and attention).
Define five feedback conditions (Contingent, att; Full-info, att; Contingent, no-att; Full-info, no-att; Random-info, att).
Run 1,000 simulation episodes per condition across 20 learning blocks and a test phase; log attention weights and choice outcomes.
Recruit human participants on Amazon Mechanical Turk.
Experiment 1: Bee classification task under contingent vs full-information feedback; record approach/avoid choices and compute 1D and 2D scores.
Experiment 2: Job-application task with baseline and three intervention variants (individuation, occlusion, noise); collect same behavioural measures.
Analyse data with Bayesian multilevel models to estimate group differences in learning trap incidence and strategy scores.
method.models: ALCOVE-RL reinforcement-learning model; Bayesian multilevel statistical models
method.outcome_variable: Incidence of attentional learning trap (e.g., high 1D score, low 2D score, misestimation of negative prospects)
method.independent_variables: Feedback condition (contingent vs full-information), presence/absence of selective attention, intervention type (individuation, occlusion, noise)

results.summary: Simulations showed that when attention learning operates under choice-contingent feedback, more than a quarter of agents converged on erroneous one-dimensional rules, whereas full feedback or disabling selective attention prevented traps. Human participants mirrored these effects: contingent-information groups relied more on one-dimensional strategies, over-estimated bad options, and adopted fewer correct two-dimensional rules than full-information groups. Debiasing interventions reduced extreme one-dimensional responding but did not meaningfully increase correct rule adoption, indicating the robustness of attentional learning traps.

numerical_results.outcome_name: Proportion of simulated agents in learning trap (Contingent, att)
Initial approach rate – human Experiment 1 (contingent vs full-information)
Estimated proportion of poor prospects (contingent participants)
